His creatinine is stable at 115 µmol/L and there is no proteinuria. Repeat biopsy shows mild C4d deposition in peritubular capillaries without histological features of rejection. What is the diagnosis and management?

options.A: Subclinical ABMR — treat with IV methylprednisolone
options.B: Chronic ABMR — intensify immunosuppression + IVIG + plasmapheresis
options.C: Subclinical DSA positivity — optimise CNI levels, monitor renal function, and repeat DSA/biopsy in 6 months
options.D: Hyperacute rejection — urgent graft nephrectomy

explanation.correct: Subclinical DSA positivity with C4d staining but no microvascular injury or clinical dysfunction represents an intermediate state — not meeting full Banff criteria for ABMR. Management: ensure adequate immunosuppression (tacrolimus trough, mycophenolate adherence), monitor creatinine and proteinuria closely, and repeat biopsy and DSA quantification at 6 months. Treating with IVIG/plasmapheresis without evidence of histological ABMR is not evidence-based.

wrong.A: Methylprednisolone is for ACR (cellular). C4d + DSA without microvascular injury is not a complete ABMR diagnosis — steroid bolus is not indicated.
wrong.B: Chronic active ABMR requires microvascular injury (glomerulitis, peritubular capillaritis), C4d, and DSA plus histological damage. Without these histological features, intensified treatment is not evidence-based and carries immunosuppression toxicity risk.
wrong.D: Hyperacute rejection is an immediate catastrophic event from pre-formed antibodies at the time of reperfusion. Stable creatinine 3 years post-transplant with new DSA detection is not hyperacute rejection.

explanation.guideline: KDIGO Transplantation Guidelines 2022 — subclinical DSA: close monitoring; full ABMR requires Banff criteria (DSA + C4d + microvascular injury)
explanation.trial: Loupy A et al., NEJM 2013: DSA and graft survival — DSA positive: 10-year graft survival 68% vs 94% DSA negative; microvascular injury on biopsy is the critical prognostic factor.
explanation.pearl: Banff 2022 ABMR criteria: (1) histological evidence of microvascular injury (glomerulitis ≥1 or peritubular capillaritis ≥1); (2) C4d positivity or evidence of endothelial activation; (3) current DSA. All 3 required for active ABMR. C4d without microvascular injury = 'C4d staining without rejection'.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man is 10 years post-deceased donor renal transplant (tacrolimus/MMF/steroids). Creatinine has been slowly rising for 2 years: 110 → 140 → 160 µmol/L. Proteinuria is 1.2g/day. Biopsy shows interstitial fibrosis and tubular atrophy (IFTA) with transplant glomerulopathy (double contour GBM), C4d in peritubular capillaries, and positive DSA anti-HLA-DQ. What is the diagnosis and management approach?

options.A: Calcineurin inhibitor nephrotoxicity — reduce tacrolimus
options.B: Chronic active antibody-mediated rejection — consider plasmapheresis, IVIG, and rituximab
options.C: Recurrent IgA nephropathy — mycophenolate dose increase
options.D: Chronic cellular rejection — steroid bolus

explanation.correct: Chronic active ABMR meeting all Banff criteria: (1) microvascular injury (transplant glomerulopathy — double contour GBM + GBM reduplication); (2) C4d in peritubular capillaries; (3) donor-specific antibody (anti-HLA-DQ). This is the leading cause of late graft loss. Management: plasmapheresis (remove circulating DSA) + IVIG + rituximab (deplete B cells). Evidence is limited but this combination is current best practice at specialist centres.

wrong.A: CNI nephrotoxicity causes interstitial fibrosis and arteriolar hyalinosis but does NOT cause transplant glomerulopathy or C4d deposition. Tacrolimus reduction alone is insufficient for ABMR.
wrong.C: Recurrent IgA nephropathy can cause proteinuria and slow graft deterioration, but shows mesangial IgA deposits on IF — not transplant glomerulopathy or C4d. MMF increase does not treat IgA recurrence.
wrong.D: Chronic cellular rejection shows tubulitis and interstitial inflammation without C4d or transplant glomerulopathy. This case has the full Banff ABMR pattern.

explanation.guideline: KDIGO Transplantation Guidelines 2022 — chronic active ABMR: plasmapheresis + IVIG ± rituximab; poor prognosis without treatment
explanation.trial: Choi J et al., J Am Soc Nephrol 2018: treatment of chronic active ABMR — plasmapheresis + IVIG ± rituximab: 50% stabilisation of graft function at 2 years; bortezomib as experimental option.
explanation.pearl: Transplant glomerulopathy (TG) on biopsy is almost pathognomonic of chronic ABMR: double contour of GBM (tram-tracking) on PASM stain from new lamina densa formation. TG is the morphological hallmark of endothelial injury from sustained antibody attack. Once TG is established, prognosis for graft survival at 5 years is ~50%.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old female renal transplant recipient plans to become pregnant. She is 2 years post-transplant, stable creatinine 100 µmol/L, no proteinuria, on tacrolimus 3mg daily, mycophenolate mofetil 1500mg daily, and prednisolone 5mg daily. What medication change is most critical before conception?

options.A: Stop tacrolimus and switch to ciclosporin
options.B: Stop mycophenolate mofetil and substitute with azathioprine
options.C: Stop prednisolone — steroids are teratogenic
options.D: No medication changes are needed — all immunosuppressants are safe in pregnancy

explanation.correct: Mycophenolate mofetil (MMF) is teratogenic (ear and limb malformations, pregnancy loss) — it must be stopped at least 6 weeks before conception and switched to azathioprine (the preferred antimetabolite in pregnancy). Tacrolimus and prednisolone are relatively safe in pregnancy at standard doses. Stable graft function (creatinine <150, no proteinuria) is a prerequisite before conception.

wrong.A: Tacrolimus can be continued in pregnancy — it is not categorically teratogenic, though dose adjustments may be needed due to altered pharmacokinetics in pregnancy. Switching to ciclosporin is not required.
wrong.C: Prednisolone is not teratogenic at doses ≤15mg daily and should be continued for immunosuppression. It does not require cessation before conception.
wrong.D: MMF is absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy. 'No changes needed' would expose the foetus to a known teratogen.

explanation.guideline: KDIGO Pregnancy After Transplant Guidelines 2021 — MMF: switch to azathioprine ≥6 weeks before conception; tacrolimus and prednisolone safe
explanation.trial: Coscia LA et al., Transplantation 2009: NTPR registry — MMF in pregnancy: 22% congenital malformations vs 4% azathioprine; MMF-exposed: microtia, cleft lip/palate, limb malformations.
explanation.pearl: Recommended immunosuppression in pregnancy: tacrolimus (adjust trough, increased clearance in pregnancy) + azathioprine (not MMF) + low-dose prednisolone. Stable graft criteria before conception: ≥1 year post-LT, creatinine <150, no rejection in past year, proteinuria <1g/day, well-controlled BP.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman has a kidney from a deceased donor with acute tubular necrosis (ATN) on biopsy. The donor creatinine at retrieval was 220 µmol/L. She develops delayed graft function. Serial biopsies at 1 and 3 weeks show persistent ATN without rejection. What additional complication must be actively screened for during DGF?

options.A: Calcineurin inhibitor toxicity masking recovery
options.B: Ureteric anastomotic stricture
options.C: Cytomegalovirus (CMV) pneumonitis
options.D: BK polyomavirus nephropathy

explanation.correct: BK polyomavirus nephropathy is an important complication to screen for during DGF because: (1) DGF requires high-level immunosuppression (to exclude rejection); (2) this creates conditions for BK reactivation; (3) BK nephropathy causes tubular injury similar to ATN on biopsy, and can be missed without BK staining (SV40) and plasma PCR. Early detection allows IS reduction before irreversible tubular destruction.

wrong.A: CNI toxicity is relevant — tacrolimus causes vasoconstriction and can impair tubular recovery. However, the question asks about complications to screen for 'actively' — BK nephropathy is specifically screened with PCR, while CNI toxicity is managed by monitoring trough levels.
wrong.B: Ureteric stricture presents with hydronephrosis and obstruction on US, causing oliguria — it is diagnosed by imaging, not biopsy. Serial biopsies showing ATN without hydronephrosis point to an intrinsic problem.
wrong.C: CMV pneumonitis is a significant post-transplant infection complication but is not specifically associated with DGF or the pattern of persistent ATN on biopsy. CMV screening is routine but not the priority in this specific scenario.

explanation.guideline: KDIGO Transplantation Guidelines 2022 — BK screening: plasma PCR monthly for 9 months, then 3-monthly to 2 years
explanation.trial: Hirsch HH et al., Am J Transplant 2019: BK nephropathy — incidence 1-10%; reduction of IS reverses nephropathy in 50%; early detection by PCR is critical for graft salvage.
explanation.pearl: BK screening strategy: plasma BK PCR thresholds: >1000 copies/mL = presumptive nephropathy → reduce IS; >10,000 copies/mL + histological damage = definitive BK nephropathy. SV40 immunostaining on biopsy is diagnostic. No approved antiviral for BK — IS reduction is the only treatment.

questions.stem: A deceased donor kidney is retrieved from a 58-year-old hypertensive donor after brain death. Donor creatinine was 135 µmol/L. The kidney is deemed marginal. Machine perfusion is available. Compared to static cold storage, what does normothermic machine perfusion (NMP) offer for marginal kidneys?

options.A: Complete elimination of delayed graft function
options.B: Reduced DGF incidence, viability assessment during preservation, and reconditioning of marginal organs
options.C: Allows preservation for up to 7 days without deterioration
options.D: Eliminates the need for immunosuppression post-transplantation

explanation.correct: Normothermic machine perfusion (NMP) of kidneys provides: (1) active metabolic support (oxygenated blood-based perfusion at 37°C) allowing organ reconditioning; (2) real-time viability assessment (urine production, lactate clearance, oxygen consumption during perfusion); (3) reduced DGF incidence compared to static cold storage, particularly for marginal (ECD/DCD) kidneys. The COMPARE trial showed hypothermic machine perfusion (HMP) reduces DGF by 50%.

wrong.A: Machine perfusion reduces but does not eliminate DGF. The incidence drops from 20-30% to 10-15% with machine perfusion for standard criteria donors, and from 50-60% to 30-40% for extended criteria donors.
wrong.C: Current machine perfusion techniques allow preservation up to 48–72 hours at most. 7-day preservation is not achievable with current technology.
wrong.D: Immunosuppression is still required for all allografts. Machine perfusion addresses preservation quality, not the immunological mismatch between donor and recipient.

explanation.guideline: KDIGO Transplantation Guidelines 2022 — hypothermic machine perfusion preferred over static cold storage for all deceased donor kidneys; NMP emerging for viability assessment
explanation.trial: COMPARE trial (Moers C, NEJM 2009): HMP vs static cold storage — DGF 20.8% vs 26.5%; 1-yr graft survival 94% vs 90%; NMP (Peters-Sengers, Lancet 2021): viability assessment changes accept/decline decision in 20% of marginal kidneys.
explanation.pearl: Machine perfusion types: HMP (hypothermic, 4°C — reduces metabolism, standard of care); NMP (normothermic, 37°C — active metabolism, viability assessment, reconditioning); SNMP (subnormothermic, 20°C — intermediate). NMP viability thresholds: urine >100mL/h, lactate <4 mmol/L, and renal vascular resistance all predict good post-transplant function.
